one_line_takeaway: A hand-curated public index of 67 deployed agentic AI systems with a 33-field documentation schema, whose headline finding is a sharp transparency gap: ~70% disclose capabilities/docs but under 20% disclose any formal safety policy and under 10% report internal or external safety testing.
key_finding: Developers are relatively forthcoming about capabilities but strikingly opaque about safety. Of the 67 indexed systems, 70.1% (47) release public documentation and 49.3% (33) release code, yet only 19.4% (13) disclose a formal safety policy, 9.0% (6) report developer-conducted safety testing, and 7.5% (5) report external safety testing/red-teaming. The few systems with formal, publicly reported safety testing come mostly from a small set of large labs (Anthropic, Google DeepMind, OpenAI). The ecosystem is also concentrated: 45/67 systems are from USA-based developers, 73.1% (49) are industry vs 26.9% (18) academic, and 74.6% (50) specialize in software engineering or computer use.
core_contribution: Introduces the AI Agent Index, described as the first public database documenting deployed agentic AI systems. The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) a structured documentation framework of 33 fields across 6 categories (basic info, developer, system components, guardrails/oversight, evaluations, ecosystem) for agentic systems; (2) the populated index itself, covering n=67 currently deployed or open-source agentic systems selected via an explicit decision graph and current as of a Dec 31, 2024 snapshot; and (3) meta-findings about the ecosystem, most notably a large asymmetry between how much developers disclose about capabilities/usage versus how little they disclose about safety, red-teaming, and risk management. Prior documentation frameworks (model cards, datasheets, reward reports, ecosystem graphs, data provenance cards, the Foundation Model Transparency Index, the AI Incident Database, the AI Risk Repository) are cited as related work that the paper builds on/contrasts with, NOT as its own contribution; the paper's claimed gap is that none of these target agentic systems specifically.
threat_model: The paper does not propose or formalize a specific adversary, attack, or defense; it is a transparency/governance-and-documentation study rather than an attack/defense paper, so no threat model is invented here. It situates itself within agentic-AI safety by cataloguing security-relevant properties: whether systems disclose formal safety policies, internal (developer-run) safety evaluations, external red-teaming/auditing, guardrails, usage/customer restrictions, and monitoring/shutdown procedures. The implicit security concern it documents is that most deployed agents ship with little to no publicly verifiable safety testing or oversight, which limits auditors', policymakers', and users' ability to reason about agent risk. Risks it references (from cited work, not measured here) include autonomous website hacking, zero-day exploitation by LLM-agent teams, loss of control, prompt injection, and physical harm from robotic agents.
limitations: By the authors' own account: (1) not exhaustive - the field is decentralized and poorly documented, so qualifying systems may be missing, and internally-used (non-public) agents are systematically under-represented; (2) English-language and Western-developer bias; (3) it is a single Dec 31, 2024 snapshot in a weekly-changing field, excluding both obsolete and post-cutoff systems (e.g., DeepSeek-R1, OpenAI Operator); (4) the 33 fields are not exhaustive and omit, e.g., real-world safety incident records; (5) only a 36% developer response rate, so absence of disclosed safety practices may reflect non-discovery rather than true absence; (6) risk of the index being 'gamed' via selective/cosmetic disclosure, which is why the authors deliberately refuse to produce developer scorecards. The 'agentic' selection threshold (higher agency than ChatGPT-4o) is admittedly subjective, and the paper explicitly declines to define 'AI agent.'

datasets_used: The AI Agent Index itself (the paper's own artifact): n=67 deployed/open-source agentic AI systems, each with a 33-field agent card, >2,200 fields total, snapshot as of Dec 31, 2024

System identification sources: web searches, academic literature, benchmark leaderboards including SWE-bench and GAIA, and third-party agent lists (aiagentslist.com, LLM-Brained-GUI-Agents survey, Letta agents-stack blog)
quantitative_results: External safety testing / red-teaming reported: 7.5% (5 of 67)
Academic openness: 16 of 18 (88.8%) academic projects release code
Domain mix: Software 37.3% and Computer use 37.3% (together 50 of 67 = 74.6%), Other 10.4%, Universal 6.0%, Research 6.0%, Robotics 3.0%
Roughly half of indexed systems were deployed in the second half of 2024
Sample agent card (Magentic One) benchmark scores as recorded in the index: GAIA 38%, AssistantBench 27.7%, WebArena 32.8%
baselines_compared: No quantitative baselines - this is a descriptive index, not a benchmarked method

Conceptual prior art contrasted as related work (not this paper's contribution): model cards, datasheets for datasets, reward reports, ecosystem graphs, data provenance cards, the Foundation Model Transparency Index, the AI Incident Database, and the AI Risk Repository; the paper argues none of these target agentic systems specifically
